reasoning: Weighted 3-year average: 1.4891. Data shows wide variance across the triangle, with older years (2001-2005) exhibiting higher factors (1.70-1.78) while more recent years (2015+) show lower factors (1.40-1.50). The simple_all of 1.7435 likely overweights sparse tail years. Selected 1.630 as a balanced blend reflecting both the stabilizing influence of recent data and the longer-term pattern. Early development is volatile; this midpoint respects that volatility while not assuming extreme outcomes. Recent years show more disciplined reporting and fewer reopened claims, supporting the lower end. Tail data quality is questionable given sparse observations.

Simple average: 1.1931. Tight clustering across all averages (1.054-1.286), with most years showing factors in the 1.15-1.23 range. The simple_all and exclude_high_low_all both support 1.19. Good consistency across both time windows and vintages. Selected 1.191 as it directly reflects observed data stability. Early claims are largely developed by age 35, with remaining development more predictable.

Weighted 10-year average: 1.1886. Tight control across all intervals; simple_all 1.1538 and weighted_10yr 1.1886 bracket the true estimate. Picked 1.189 (weighted 10-year) as it reflects the recent stable period while respecting full history. Development continues but moderates; claim severity and closure patterns stabilize.

Weighted all: 1.0492. Remarkable stability across all methods: simple_all 1.0486, simple_10yr 1.053, weighted_all 1.0492. CV very low (0.0355). Selected 1.049 (weighted_all) reflecting consensus. By age 47, claims are largely reported and closed; remaining development is dominated by reopens and legal/medical inflation on a smaller subset.

Simple all: 1.0620. Range across averages spans 1.0543-1.1327, with weighted methods tending higher (1.0752-1.1167). Data shows uptick relative to adjacent intervals. Selected 1.078 as a blend of simple_all (1.062) and weighted methods, reflecting genuine but modest elevation. CV is 0.0934, indicating real dispersion. Likely driven by reopened claim activity and settlement inflation patterns on tail.
